refined_fr.refined_fr: Capture and represent worksite spatial features in a machine-readable model.
refined_fr.rationale: The candidate FR bundles three distinct obligations: (1) terrain analysis, (2) obstacle identification, and (3) work-zone representation. Each is a separate functional concern with different data sources, processing logic, and stakeholder consumption patterns. The refined FR is solution-neutral (avoids 'mapping/modeling' terminology which implies a specific DP) and focuses on the core problem: making spatial features available in a structured, queryable form. Splitting into atomic sub-FRs allows independent verification and design of each capability.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Confirm that a worksite model exists in a defined schema/format, contains spatial coordinates and feature classifications, and is accessible to downstream planning and execution functions per acceptance criteria.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-006.1: Acquire terrain elevation and surface properties across the worksite.
FR-NGHE-006.2: Identify and locate static obstacles (structures, vegetation, utilities) within the worksite boundary.
FR-NGHE-006.3: Define and represent active work zones and their operational constraints.
FR-NGHE-006.4: Persist and retrieve worksite spatial model for planning and execution functions.
refined_fr.note: The candidate FR is not atomic; it has been decomposed into four atomic sub-FRs, each addressing a single functional obligation without internal 'and'/'or' logic.

candidate_dps: DP1: Acquire spatial feature data through multi-modal sensing (vision, range, inertial) and fuse into a unified coordinate frame; store as a structured geometric model (point clouds, meshes, or voxel grids) with semantic labels and metadata; expose via query interface for downstream planning/execution consumers.

DP2: Decompose worksite into discrete zones; assign each zone a feature inventory (obstacles, hazards, access points) via manual annotation or semi-automated detection; represent as a graph of zone nodes with edge constraints; persist in a queryable schema.

DP3: Capture worksite features as a rule-based symbolic representation (e.g., logic rules or ontology) that encodes spatial relationships, safety constraints, and operational policies; derive a queryable model on demand by evaluating rules against sensor observations.

DP4: Establish a real-time streaming pipeline that continuously ingests sensor data, applies feature extraction and classification, and maintains an incrementally updated spatial model; expose model state via subscription/polling interface for planning/execution.

DP5: Delegate feature capture to a hybrid approach: use pre-defined templates (e.g., site layout blueprints, CAD models) as a baseline; augment with live sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features; merge into a composite model.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Hybrid baseline-augmented model. Establish a composite spatial representation by anchoring to pre-defined worksite templates (blueprints, CAD, or prior maps) and augmenting with live multi-modal sensor observations to detect deviations, dynamic hazards, and unplanned features. Fuse baseline and live data into a unified, queryable geometric and semantic model. Expose model state and feature queries via a standardized interface for planning and execution consumers. Maintain model freshness and consistency through incremental updates and conflict resolution policies aligned with safety and operational constraints.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between sensor modality selection and feature extraction logic—mitigated by abstracting sensor input as a generic observation stream and keeping extraction logic modular.

Dependency on baseline template availability and accuracy—mitigated by graceful degradation: if baseline unavailable, fall back to DP1 (pure sensor fusion); if baseline inaccurate, detection of deviations triggers re-calibration.
Coupling between model update frequency and planning/execution cycle time—mitigated by decoupling via asynchronous query interface; consumers poll or subscribe at their own cadence.
Potential coupling to specific safety/operational policy encoding—mitigated by externalizing policies as queryable constraints rather than hard-coding into model structure.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 achieves loose coupling by separating concerns: baseline provision (external), live sensing (pluggable), fusion logic (centralized), and policy enforcement (declarative). The hybrid approach reduces brittleness compared to pure sensor fusion (DP1) or pure symbolic reasoning (DP3), and supports incremental model refinement without full re-acquisition.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Worksite baseline (blueprint, CAD, or prior map) is available and reasonably accurate at mission start. RISK: If baseline is stale, missing, or significantly inaccurate, fusion may produce misleading composite model. MITIGATION: Implement baseline validation checks; flag high-confidence deviations; support manual override.

ASSUMPTION: Multi-modal sensor suite (vision, range, inertial) is available and calibrated. RISK: Sensor failures, miscalibration, or occlusions degrade feature detection. MITIGATION: Implement sensor health monitoring; use redundancy where safety-critical; graceful degradation to available modalities.

ASSUMPTION: Feature extraction and semantic labeling can be automated or semi-automated. RISK: Misclassification of hazards or features leads to unsafe planning. MITIGATION: Conservative labeling (over-report hazards); human-in-the-loop validation for high-risk features; traceability of labeling confidence.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies are formalized and queryable. RISK: Policies are ambiguous, incomplete, or conflict with model representation. MITIGATION: Explicit policy schema; validation against model; escalation for unresolvable conflicts.

ASSUMPTION: Planning and execution functions can tolerate model latency and eventual consistency. RISK: Stale model leads to unsafe or inefficient decisions. MITIGATION: Define acceptable model age; implement update prioritization; alert consumers to model freshness.

ASSUMPTION: Composite model can be efficiently queried and updated in real-time. RISK: Model complexity or update frequency exceeds computational budget. MITIGATION: Hierarchical model representation; lazy evaluation; caching; spatial indexing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP5 to explicitly separate three sub-concerns: (1) Baseline provisioning and validation (DP5a), (2) Live feature detection and anomaly flagging (DP5b), and (3) Fusion, conflict resolution, and query interface (DP5c). This decomposition clarifies ownership boundaries (IM layer) and allows independent evolution of baseline sources, sensor pipelines, and model consumers. Additionally, introduce a formal model schema (e.g., GeoJSON + semantic tags + policy constraints) to ensure interoperability and auditability. Define acceptance criteria for model freshness, feature detection recall/precision, and policy compliance.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized spatial representation engine that maintains hybrid baseline-augmented models, manages sensor fusion, handles baseline-to-live data reconciliation, and exposes unified geometric/semantic queries to planning and execution consumers.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingest multi-modal sensor observations (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS) from Vehicle and Teleoperation to augment baseline model with live data.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Expose queryable model state and feature queries (geometric, semantic, deviation detection) to planning and execution consumers for mission adaptation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emit alerts for detected dynamic hazards, unplanned features, and baseline deviations that violate safety constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Receive baseline templates (blueprints, CAD, prior maps) and synchronize model state across distributed agents; handle incremental updates and conflict resolution.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for spatial representation, sensor fusion, baseline management, and model queries. All core DP logic (hybrid model, fusion, conflict resolution, query interface) resides here. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and safety alert emission cross boundaries via standard interfaces.

Poor fit. Vehicle is a platform/agent, not a representation engine. Placing hybrid model logic here couples the model tightly to one vehicle instance, breaks multi-agent sharing, and forces duplication across multiple vehicles. Baseline management and conflict resolution would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.

Poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a spatial representation layer. Model fusion and baseline reconciliation are orthogonal to teleoperation concerns. High diffusion: model state queries and hazard detection would leak into Vehicle and Safety Services.

Weak fit. Safety Services consumes model state and hazard alerts but should not own the model itself. Placing hybrid model logic here conflates representation with safety policy enforcement. Baseline management, sensor fusion, and query logic would diffuse to NGHE Solution and Vehicle. Safety Services should subscribe to SafetyAlert, not generate it from internal model state.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the single authoritative owner of the hybrid baseline-augmented spatial model. It ingests multi-modal telemetry, maintains baseline templates, performs sensor fusion and conflict resolution, and exposes a unified queryable interface. Vehicle and Teleoperation consume model queries via Tasking; Safety Services subscribes to SafetyAlert. This design centralizes representation logic, eliminates duplication, and ensures model consistency across all consumers.
containment_risks: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck if model updates are synchronous and high-frequency; recommend asynchronous, event-driven updates via Network Link.
Conflict resolution policies must be pre-defined and aligned with safety constraints; ambiguous or ad-hoc policies will cause diffusion of decision logic to Safety Services.

If baseline templates are large or heterogeneous (CAD, blueprints, prior maps), template ingestion and normalization logic must be encapsulated within NGHE Solution to avoid leakage to Vehicle or Teleoperation.
Query interface must be well-specified and versioned; vague or evolving query semantics will cause consumers (planning, execution) to implement local workarounds, diffusing model logic.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Acceptance criteria explicitly require 'worksite model/map generated and available to planning/execution functions for agreed set of site features.' NGHE Solution's Tasking interface (out) directly satisfies this by exposing queryable model state and feature queries. Verification is measurable: (1) model availability via Tasking API, (2) feature coverage against agreed site feature list, (3) latency/freshness SLAs for incremental updates. Safety plan alignment is verifiable via SafetyAlert interface emission and conflict resolution policy audit.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR maps unambiguously to NGHE Solution as single authoritative owner. Allocation is explicit: NGHE Solution owns hybrid model logic, sensor fusion, baseline reconciliation, and query interface. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are consumers, not owners. Ownership boundary is well-defined and non-overlapping.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 (Hybrid baseline-augmented model) directly and completely addresses FR (capture and represent worksite spatial features in machine-readable model). DP logic covers all required elements: (1) baseline anchoring (blueprints, CAD, prior maps), (2) live multi-modal sensor augmentation (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS), (3) deviation and hazard detection, (4) unified geometric/semantic representation, (5) queryable interface for consumers, (6) incremental updates and conflict resolution. DP is solution-complete and logically sound. No functional gaps identified.

diffusion_risks: Conflict resolution policies must be pre-defined and formally documented within NGHE Solution; if policies are ambiguous or deferred to runtime, decision logic will diffuse to Safety Services and Vehicle, creating inconsistent model state across consumers.

Baseline template ingestion and normalization (CAD, blueprints, prior maps) must be fully encapsulated within NGHE Solution; if template parsing or schema mapping leaks to Vehicle or Teleoperation, heterogeneous baseline handling will fragment across modules.

Query interface semantics (geometric, semantic, deviation detection) must be precisely specified and versioned; vague or evolving query contracts will cause planning and execution consumers to implement local workarounds and duplicate model logic.

Synchronous model update paths via Network Link may create bottlenecks under high-frequency sensor ingestion; recommend asynchronous, event-driven architecture with explicit backpressure handling to prevent diffusion of update logic to Vehicle or Teleoperation.

SafetyAlert emission criteria and thresholds must be owned by NGHE Solution, not Safety Services; if Safety Services re-evaluates or filters alerts, hazard detection logic will diffuse and create inconsistent safety posture.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment (0.95 score). NGHE Solution is the natural and correct home for DP5. All core logic (hybrid model, sensor fusion, baseline management, conflict resolution, query interface) is contained within a single module boundary. Telemetry ingestion and SafetyAlert emission are appropriate cross-boundary flows via standard interfaces; they do not represent diffusion because they are unidirectional, well-defined, and do not leak representation logic. Vehicle (0.15), Teleoperation (0.2), and Safety Services (0.3) are correctly positioned as consumers, not owners. No significant diffusion detected at current design level. Recommended action: formalize conflict resolution policies, baseline template handling, and query interface specification as design artifacts to prevent future diffusion during implementation.

recommended_revisions: Formalize conflict resolution policy specification as a design document within NGHE Solution scope. Define precedence rules (baseline vs. live data), temporal windows for reconciliation, and safety constraint alignment. Make this policy auditable and version-controlled.

Specify baseline template schema and normalization logic as a contained sub-module within NGHE Solution. Document supported formats (CAD, blueprint, prior map) and transformation rules to unified internal representation. Prevent Vehicle or Teleoperation from performing ad-hoc template parsing.

Define and version the Tasking interface query contract (geometric, semantic, deviation detection). Specify query response schemas, latency SLAs, and consistency guarantees. Publish as API specification to prevent consumer workarounds.

Document SafetyAlert emission criteria and thresholds as owned by NGHE Solution. Specify which deviations, hazards, and baseline violations trigger alerts. Prevent Safety Services from re-evaluating or filtering alerts; Safety Services should only consume and act on alerts.

Recommend asynchronous, event-driven architecture for Telemetry ingestion and Network Link synchronization. Define backpressure handling and update ordering to prevent bottlenecks and ensure model consistency under high-frequency sensor streams.

Add explicit acceptance test: verify that worksite model is queryable via Tasking interface for all agreed site features within 5 seconds of sensor observation (or per operational SLA). Verify SafetyAlert is emitted within safety-critical latency bounds.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR-DP-IM alignment with high confidence (all three gates pass). However, diffusion risks and containment notes identify critical design artifacts that must be formalized at the next decomposition level to prevent logic leakage during implementation. Safety criticality (high) and the complexity of conflict resolution, baseline normalization, and query interface semantics justify decomposing DP5 into sub-FRs that explicitly own these concerns. Proceeding to next level ensures that containment is preserved through formal specification and ownership clarity at finer granularity.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Establish and maintain authoritative baseline model from heterogeneous source formats (CAD, blueprints, prior maps) with normalized geometric and semantic representation.
FR5.2: Ingest, fuse, and reconcile multi-modal sensor telemetry (LiDAR, camera, IMU, GPS) with baseline model to detect deviations and hazards in real-time.
FR5.3: Resolve conflicts between baseline and live sensor data according to pre-defined precedence rules and temporal reconciliation windows, ensuring safety constraint alignment.
FR5.4: Emit SafetyAlert notifications for detected deviations, hazards, and baseline violations according to owned criteria and thresholds, with safety-critical latency guarantees.

FR5.5: Provide queryable interface (Tasking) for planning and execution consumers to access worksite model state, feature geometry, semantic attributes, and deviation status with specified latency and consistency SLAs.

revise.why: DP5 is logically sound but requires explicit sub-DP articulation for conflict resolution policy, baseline template handling, SafetyAlert ownership, and query interface specification. Current DP5 is solution-complete at conceptual level but lacks the design detail needed to prevent diffusion during implementation. Sub-DPs should formalize: (1) conflict resolution precedence and temporal windows, (2) baseline schema and normalization rules, (3) SafetyAlert emission criteria as owned by NGHE Solution (not Safety Services), (4) Tasking query contract with response schemas and SLAs, (5) asynchronous event-driven architecture for telemetry ingestion with backpressure handling.

assumptions: Conflict resolution policies can be pre-defined and formalized before implementation; runtime policy discovery is not acceptable.
Baseline template formats (CAD, blueprints, prior maps) are known and finite; ad-hoc format support will not be required post-deployment.
SafetyAlert emission is a pure NGHE Solution responsibility; Safety Services will not re-evaluate or filter alerts.
Tasking query interface can be versioned and published as a stable API contract; consumer workarounds are preventable through clear specification.
Network Link and Telemetry ingestion can support asynchronous, event-driven patterns; synchronous blocking is not a hard constraint.
Safety-critical latency bounds for SafetyAlert emission are defined and achievable within NGHE Solution architecture (recommend <500ms for hazard detection).
open_risks: Conflict resolution policy ambiguity: if precedence rules (baseline vs. live data) are not formally documented, Safety Services or Vehicle may implement local reconciliation logic, fragmenting model state.
Baseline template schema evolution: if supported formats or transformation rules change post-deployment, Vehicle or Teleoperation may perform ad-hoc parsing, leaking normalization logic.
Query interface contract drift: if Tasking interface semantics are not versioned and published, consumers will implement local workarounds and duplicate model logic.
SafetyAlert ownership diffusion: if Safety Services re-evaluates or filters alerts, hazard detection logic will diffuse and create inconsistent safety posture across modules.

Telemetry ingestion bottleneck: if Network Link synchronization is synchronous and blocking, high-frequency sensor streams may cause model update delays, forcing Vehicle or Teleoperation to cache stale state.
Latency SLA non-compliance: if SafetyAlert emission or Tasking query response latency exceeds safety-critical bounds, planning and execution may fail or operate on outdated information.
evidence_needed: Formal conflict resolution policy specification document (precedence rules, temporal windows, safety constraint alignment) owned by NGHE Solution.
Baseline template schema definition and normalization logic specification (supported formats, transformation rules, version control).
Tasking interface API specification (query contract, response schemas, latency SLAs, consistency guarantees).
SafetyAlert emission criteria and threshold specification (owned by NGHE Solution, not Safety Services).
Architecture design document for asynchronous, event-driven telemetry ingestion with backpressure handling.

Acceptance test plan: verify worksite model queryability for all agreed site features within 5 seconds (or per operational SLA); verify SafetyAlert latency within safety-critical bounds (<500ms recommended).
Verification of conflict resolution policy audit trail and version control.
Verification that Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services do not implement local baseline parsing, conflict resolution, or SafetyAlert filtering.
